request.question: Invasion Attack included title matches by the Japanese sumo wrestler from what Hawaiian city?

title: Takanonami Sadahiro
Endō Shōta
Akebono Tarō
Aminishiki Ryūji
Kotoōshū Katsunori
Jōkōryū Takayuki
Yamamotoyama Ryūta
Invasion Attack (2013)
Kasugafuji Akihiro
Kaiketsu Masateru
sentences: Takanonami Sadahiro (born Sadahiro Namioka October 27, 1971 – June 20, 2015) was a Japanese sumo wrestler and coach from Aomori.
He held sumo's second highest rank of "ōzeki" from 1994 until 2000.
He won two tournament championships, and was a runner-up in eight others.
He was a sumo coach from his retirement in 2004 until his death in 2015 at the age of 43.
Endō Shōta (遠藤 聖大 ) , (born October 19, 1990) is a professional sumo wrestler from Ishikawa Prefecture, Japan.
After a successful amateur career, he turned professional in March 2013, making the top "makuuchi" division that September.
His highest rank has been "maegashira" 1, and he has two special prizes for Fighting Spirit and Technique, and three gold stars for defeating "yokozuna."
He was runner-up in the September 2016 tournament.
He wrestles for Oitekaze stable.
He is extremely popular with Japanese sumo fans and has been regarded as one of the most promising home-grown wrestlers in sumo.
Akebono Tarō (曙 太郎 , Akebono Tarō , born Chadwick Haheo Rowan; 8 May 1969) is an American-born Japanese professional wrestler and former sumo wrestler from Waimānalo, Hawaii.
Joining the professional sport in Japan in 1988, he was trained by pioneering Hawaiian sumo wrestler Takamiyama and rose swiftly up the rankings, reaching the top division in 1990.

After two consecutive "yusho" or tournament championships in November 1992 and January 1993 he made history by becoming the first non-Japanese-born wrestler ever to reach "yokozuna", the highest rank in sumo.
Aminishiki Ryūji (born October 3, 1978 as Ryūji Suginomori) is a Japanese sumo wrestler.
He made his professional debut in 1997 and reached the top "makuuchi" division in 2000.
He has earned eleven special prizes and won eight "kinboshi" or gold stars for defeating "yokozuna" during his career, which for some time was twice as many as any other active wrestler.
He has twice been runner-up in a tournament.
The highest rank he has reached is "sekiwake".
He is in the all-time top ten for a number of sumo records, including most top division wins, most top division appearances and most tournaments ranked in the top division.
He wrestles for Isegahama stable.

Kotoōshū Katsunori (琴欧洲 勝紀) (legal name: Karoyan Andō, born February 19, 1983 as Kaloyan Stefanov Mahlyanov, Bulgarian: Калоян Стефанов Махлянов , in Dzhulyunitsa, Veliko Tarnovo Province, Bulgaria) is a former professional sumo wrestler or "rikishi".
He made his debut in 2002, reaching the top division just two years later.
In 2005 he reached the rank of "ōzeki" or 'champion', the second-highest level in the sumo ranking system behind only "yokozuna."
On May 24, 2008, Kotoōshū made history by becoming the first European sumo wrestler to win an Emperor's Cup.
He was one of the longest serving "ōzeki" in sumo history, holding the rank for 47 consecutive tournaments until November 2013.
In January 2014 Kotoōshū obtained Japanese citizenship, a requirement of becoming an elder in the Japan Sumo Association, and he announced his retirement during the following tournament in March.
In April 2017 he opened his own training stable, Naruto.
Jōkōryū Takayuki (born August 7, 1988 as Takayuki Sakuma), formerly known as Sakumayama Takayuki, is a Japanese sumo wrestler.
He made his professional debut in 2011 after a successful college career in sumo.
He currently holds the record for the most consecutive wins since entering professional sumo, and for the fastest rise to the top "makuuchi" division from the lowest "jonokuchi" division.
His highest rank has been "komusubi", which he reached in September 2014.
However, because of injuries he has fallen down the rankings, reaching a low of "sandanme" 23 in November 2016.

Ryūichi Yamamoto (山本 龍一 , Yamamoto Ryūichi , born May 8, 1984) , known by his "shikona" Yamamotoyama Ryūta (山本山 龍太 ) , or simply Yama, is a Japanese retired sumo wrestler from the city of Saitama in Saitama Prefecture.
He made his professional debut in January 2007, and reached the top "makuuchi" division in January 2009.
His highest rank was "maegashira" 9.
At 265 kg , Yamamotoyama is the heaviest Japanese-born sumo wrestler in history, and is also thought to be the heaviest Japanese person ever.
In April 2011, he was told to retire by the Japan Sumo Association after he and several other wrestlers were found to be involved in match-fixing.
He now lives in Los Angeles and participates in sumo exhibitions and amateur tournaments, often alongside Ulambayaryn Byambajav.
Invasion Attack was a professional wrestling pay-per-view (PPV) promoted by New Japan Pro-Wrestling (NJPW).
The event took place on April 7, 2013, in Tokyo at Ryōgoku Kokugikan.
The event featured nine matches, five of which were contested for championships.

As part of the "invasion" theme, the event featured title matches involving championships from Consejo Mundial de Lucha Libre (CMLL) and the National Wrestling Alliance (NWA) as well as participation from outside workers Akebono and Bob Sapp.
It was the first event under the Invasion Attack name.
Kasugafuji Akihiro (February 20, 1966 – March 9, 2017), born as Shoki Iwanaga, was a Japanese sumo wrestler and coach from Oshika, Miyagi.
He was an active wrestler in professional sumo from 1981 until 1996, reaching a highest rank of "maegashira" 1.
After his retirement he re-established the Kasugayama stable in 1997 and trained his own wrestlers.

He left the Japan Sumo Association in 2012 after an expenses scandal, and was involved with a legal dispute in 2013 with his successor as head of Kasugayama stable which was not resolved until shortly before his death in 2017.
Kaiketsu Masateru (Japanese: 魁傑 將晃, born Teruyuki Nishimori; February 16, 1948 – May 18, 2014) was a Japanese sumo wrestler, who reached the second highest rank of "ōzeki" on two separate occasions.
He also won two top division tournament championships.
After his retirement in 1979 he became a coach under the name of Hanaregoma-oyakata and established Hanaregoma stable.
He was also chairman of the Japan Sumo Association from 2010 to 2012.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Produce a compact, structured first-hop summary that helps the next agent fetch the missing second-hop facts. The summary must be one labeled block with these four short sections (each 1–4 lines):

1) Evidence found — For each concrete target term or name mentioned in the passages, list the target and which passage(s) mention it, and quote a short verbatim excerpt (≤15 words) or any exact numeric/date token from the passage. Preserve the quoted text and any numeric/date formatting exactly as it appears in the passages. Include passage identifier(s) exactly as given (title or index).

2) Ambiguities — Enumerate any names or terms that correspond to multiple candidate pages or where the passages return multiple variants or conflicting values. For each ambiguous item, list the candidate labels or note the conflict using only tokens from the passages where possible.

3) Bridge / missing fact — State exactly which relation(s) or fact(s) needed to answer the question are not present or are unresolved in the provided passages (e.g., birth date, publication year, author of adaptation, founding year, award count). Be explicit and concise.

4) Retrieval direction — Provide 2–4 concrete next-hop retrievals: specific page titles or disambiguation pages, or precise search queries the next agent should run to fetch the missing bridge facts. Use actionable, precise phrasing (page title or quoted query). End this section with an explicit deferral of the final answer when the passages do not unambiguously supply all required facts.

Formatting and behavior rules:
- Produce only the single field `summary` as the output. The `summary` text must follow the four labeled sections above.
- Always quote verbatim evidence (names, short excerpts, dates, numbers) exactly as in the passages; do not reformat numeric/date tokens.
- Do NOT produce a final answer here unless every required fact to resolve the question is present and unambiguous in the provided passages (i.e., each named entity corresponds to a single candidate and the needed relation value is explicitly present). If facts are unambiguous and complete, you may state that and include a concise final conclusion in the `summary`; otherwise explicitly defer (for example: "Final answer deferred pending retrieval of the targets below.").
- If the natural summary would otherwise be a single bare token or name, append a one-line bridge clause that identifies the missing relation and at least one retrieval target rather than ending with the bare token.
- Keep phrasing concise and factual; favor short lines, source-linked evidence excerpts, and explicit next-step instructions. Do not attempt to fetch pages yourself—only list retrieval targets/queries.

Example structure to follow in the `summary` field (use passage text and titles exactly):
Evidence found: ...
Ambiguities: ...
Bridge / missing fact: ...
Retrieval direction: ...

End of instructions.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Required output format and constraints:
- Produce exactly one field named `query`. Its value must be a single short, keyword-style search string (no full sentences, no explanations, no assertions, no user-facing clarifications).
- The query must be terse (preferably <= 12 words) and contain no clause punctuation or sentence grammar (no question marks, no leading "who/what is", no verbs forming assertions).
- Use only tokens that are visible in `question` and/or `summary_1`, except you may insert at most one minimal neutral connector token to target bridging evidence (allowed connector tokens include, but are not limited to: artist, album, director, who directed, biography, introduction, genre, regular season). Do not invent new entity names or facts.
- When the inputs present multiple entities or candidate items that must be linked (for example two titles, two names, or an anchor title plus a candidate), include both entity tokens from the inputs and a single neutral connector token between or adjacent to them to indicate the evidence bridge to retrieve (e.g., "EntityA EntityB director").
- If the question or summary includes structured tokens relevant to the bridge (years, league IDs, edition names), include those tokens plus a concise cue (for example year + league + "regular season") as part of the short query.
- You may perform light normalization of obvious misspellings in entity names or technical terms drawn from the visible inputs (fix clear typos), but do not invent alternative candidate entities or assert corrected identities beyond the token-level fix.
- If it is not possible to form a fully disambiguating query from visible tokens, still emit the best compact retrieval query possible using only visible tokens and an allowed connector; do not ask the user for clarification at this step. (Downstream retrieval will be attempted first; user clarification is allowed only after retrieval fails.)

Produce only the `query` field value (the single short search string) and nothing else.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single text field only):
- Output exactly one concise summary string (no extra fields, labels, or metadata). Keep the summary to 1–2 short sentences unless the evidence forces a single-token identification (see below).
- Primary goal: integrate the first-hop identity in `context` with facts found in `passages` so the final answer agent has the explicit, supported linkage it needs.

When the question requires linking the context entity to information in the passages and the passages supply the bridging fact:
- Produce a concise (1–2 sentence) integrated summary that (1) names the context entity (using the form present in `context`), (2) names the supporting entity/attribute found in `passages` (using the form present in the inputs), and (3) states the explicit relation or fact (membership, creator attribution, partnership, founding/establishment, award, etc.) exactly as supported by the inputs. Use only facts present in `context` and `passages` and avoid introducing any unsupported details.

Name and attribution formatting:
- If a canonical multi-token name (given + family name or full official name) appears in the inputs, use that full form in the summary; do not truncate to a surname or partial name.
- For questions about creator/author/credit, prefer the person-name attribution as given in the inputs over returning the title of a work or event.

Identification requests:
- If the question explicitly asks for a single identification (for example, asks "which X" or otherwise requests a named token or label), return exactly the canonical name(s) as presented in the passages. If multiple valid names are requested, return them comma-separated. Do not add explanatory clauses, relational fragments, or extra text in these cases—only the canonical name(s).

When evidence is missing or ambiguous:
- If the passages do not supply a clear bridging fact, state that succinctly (e.g., "No supporting evidence in the provided passages" or "Unclear from the provided sources"). Retain uncertainty rather than asserting unsupported facts.

Conciseness and fidelity constraints:
- Keep the summary short and factual (1–2 sentences). Do not expand into long biographies, exhaustive lists, or unrelated details.
- Do not assert facts that are not present in `context` and `passages`.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passage text, but short verbatim names or phrases necessary for fidelity are allowed.
- Treat the `context` field as the primary first-hop identity to be linked to `passages` when integrating evidence.

Produce the `summary` now based only on the supplied `question`, `context`, and `passages`.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Use summary_1 and summary_2 as the sole evidential basis for the final answer; give greatest weight to summary_2 (the later, post-retrieval integrated evidence). Do not introduce facts from outside these summaries.

Output rules (apply in order):

1) If summary_1 and summary_2 clearly and unambiguously agree on the same concise answer, set the value of `answer` to exactly one minimal string and nothing else:
   - For a binary yes/no conclusion, emit the single lowercase token: yes or no.
   - For any other concise label, emit that label exactly as it appears in the summaries (trim leading/trailing whitespace and strip any surrounding punctuation or wrapper syntax such as surrounding parentheses, quotes, or JSON-like braces). Do not add words, sentences, punctuation, quotes, brackets, or any other surrounding text.

2) Preserve multi-token labels: if any visible summary contains a multi-token form of the answer (for example a full personal name, a multi-word company or place), output that full multi-token form verbatim. Only shorten a label if no multi-token form appears in either summary.

3) Company/developer preference: when the question requests a company or developer and the summaries show a subordinate studio together with an explicit parent/publisher label (e.g., in parentheses or with phrasing like "published by"), choose the parent/publisher name as the single minimal answer string. If no parent/publisher is explicitly indicated, choose the best-supported developer/studio name from the summaries.

4) If the summaries conflict or are ambiguous, do NOT apply the unanimous-agreement rule in (1). Instead, use summary_2 as the primary basis and emit the minimal concise string supported by summary_2, applying rules (2) and (3) as relevant. If summary_2 provides no concise supported answer, set `answer` to the minimal explicit token "unknown".

Formatting constraints (must always be followed):
- Produce exactly one field: `answer`.
- The value of `answer` must be a single minimal string (no surrounding text, punctuation, explanation, or structural wrappers).
- Do not output extra fields, labels, or markup.

Additional guidance:
- If summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge but summary_2 provides a resolved, supported conclusion, use the conclusion from summary_2.
- If a concise answer string can be drawn verbatim from the summaries, prefer that verbatim text over paraphrase.

End.

response.hop1_query: Invasion Attack included title matches by the Japanese sumo wrestler from what Hawaiian city?
hop1_docs: Akebono Tarō | Akebono Tarō (曙 太郎 , Akebono Tarō , born Chadwick Haheo Rowan; 8 May 1969) is an American-born Japanese professional wrestler and former sumo wrestler from Waimānalo, Hawaii.  Joining the professional sport in Japan in 1988, he was trained by pioneering Hawaiian sumo wrestler Takamiyama and rose swiftly up the rankings, reaching the top division in 1990.  After two consecutive "yusho" or tournament championships in November 1992 and January 1993 he made history by becoming the first non-Japanese-born wrestler ever to reach "yokozuna", the highest rank in sumo.

Yamamotoyama Ryūta | Ryūichi Yamamoto (山本 龍一 , Yamamoto Ryūichi , born May 8, 1984) , known by his "shikona" Yamamotoyama Ryūta (山本山 龍太 ) , or simply Yama, is a Japanese retired sumo wrestler from the city of Saitama in Saitama Prefecture.  He made his professional debut in January 2007, and reached the top "makuuchi" division in January 2009.  His highest rank was "maegashira" 9.  At 265 kg , Yamamotoyama is the heaviest Japanese-born sumo wrestler in history, and is also thought to be the heaviest Japanese person ever.  In April 2011, he was told to retire by the Japan Sumo Association after he and several other wrestlers were found to be involved in match-fixing.  He now lives in Los Angeles and participates in sumo exhibitions and amateur tournaments, often alongside Ulambayaryn Byambajav.

NJPW Invasion Tour 2011 | NJPW Invasion Tour 2011: Attack on East Coast was the first American tour run by Japanese professional wrestling promotion New Japan Pro-Wrestling (NJPW) in May 2011.  The tour was hosted by Jersey All Pro Wrestling (JAPW) and featured all of NJPW's top wrestlers, matches for three of the promotion's four championships and the introduction of a new fifth title.

Magaki stable | Magaki stable (間垣部屋 , Magaki beya ) was a stable of sumo wrestlers, formerly one of the Nishonoseki group of stables.  Wakanohana Kanji II, the 56th Yokozuna in sumo history, re-established the stable in 1983.  Its first wrestler to reach the top "makuuchi" division was the Hawaiian born Yamato in 1997, followed by Gojōrō and Wakanojō, also in 1997.  However the stable has had less success in recent years.  Russian "maegashira" Wakanohō was thrown out of sumo in 2008 after being accused of cannabis possession, charges which were eventually dropped.  In 2011, its highest ranked wrestler Wakatenrō was forced to retire because of accusations of match-fixing which he admitted to after being banned from competition.

Kasugaō Katsumasa | Kasugaō Katsumasa 春日王 克昌 (born July 1, 1977 as Kim Sung Tak 김성택) is a former sumo wrestler from Incheon, South Korea.  He was the first sumo wrestler to reach the top "makuuchi" division officially representing South Korea.  (Several other top wrestlers in the past have hid a Korean or half-Korean background).  He joined sumo in 1998, making the top division for the first time in 2003.  His highest rank was "maegashira" 3.  In September 2009, he acquired Japanese citizenship.  In April 2011 he was ordered to retire by the Japan Sumo Association after an investigation found him guilty of match-fixing.

Konishiki Yasokichi | Konishiki Yasokichi (小錦八十吉 , Konishiki Yasokichi , born Saleva'a Fuauli Atisano'e; December 31, 1963) is a Hawaiian-born Japanese-Samoan former sumo wrestler.  He was the first non-Japanese-born wrestler to reach "ōzeki", the second-highest rank in the sport.  During his career he won the top division championship on three occasions and came close to becoming the first foreign-born grand champion, or "yokozuna", prompting a debate as to whether a foreigner could have the necessary cultural understanding to be acceptable in sumo's ultimate rank.  At a peak weight of 287 kg he was also the heaviest "rikishi" ever in sumo, earning him the nicknames "Meat Bomb" and "Dump Truck".

Toyonishiki Kiichiro | Toyonishiki Kiichiro (born Harley Ozaki, 3 February 1920 – 26 September 1998) was a Japanese-American sumo wrestler from Pierce, Colorado who was one of the first foreign-born wrestlers to reach the top "makuuchi" division.  He made his professional debut in January 1938 and reached "makuuchi" in May 1944, nearly 25 years before the more well known Hawaiian born Takamiyama Daigoro.  However, he fought in only one tournament in the top division before being drafted into the Imperial Japanese Army against his wishes, and he was never returned to sumo.  He remained in Japan after the war, and ran an inn later in life.
response.summary_1:

- Akebono Tarō — [1]: "from Waimānalo, Hawaii."
- Akebono Tarō — [1]: "8 May 1969"
- Yamamotoyama Ryūta — [2]: "a Japanese retired sumo wrestler from the city of Saitama"
- Konishiki Yasokichi — [6]: "Hawaiian-born Japanese-Samoan former sumo wrestler."
- NJPW Invasion Tour 2011 — [3]: "NJPW Invasion Tour 2011: Attack on East Coast"
- Magaki stable — [4]: "the Hawaiian born Yamato in 1997"

Ambiguities:
- "Invasion Attack" vs. "NJPW Invasion Tour 2011: Attack on East Coast" (passages show "Invasion Tour 2011", not "Invasion Attack").
- "the Japanese sumo wrestler" could refer to multiple candidates in passages: "Akebono Tarō", "Konishiki Yasokichi", or other Hawaiian-born wrestlers like "Yamato".
- Several Hawaiian-born references exist but only Akebono's passage gives a specific city ("Waimānalo, Hawaii."); others give national/ethnic origin without city.

Bridge / missing fact:
- No passage links any "Invasion Attack" event to a wrestler's title matches.
- Missing confirmation of which wrestler had "title matches" at "Invasion Attack" and which event-year "Invasion Attack" refers to.
- If the wrestler is Akebono, we still lack any passage showing Akebono had title matches at an event named "Invasion Attack."

Retrieval direction:
1) Open the event page "Invasion Attack (NJPW) - Wikipedia" or search "Invasion Attack - Wikipedia" to find the correct year(s) and match cards.
2) Search the event-specific pages: "Invasion Attack 2013 - Wikipedia", "Invasion Attack 2014 - Wikipedia", "Invasion Attack 2015 - Wikipedia" (or list of Invasion Attack events) to inspect match cards for "title match" participants.
3) Query "Invasion Attack Akebono" and "Invasion Attack Konishiki" (quoted) to locate any mention of those wrestlers on the Invasion Attack cards.
4) If a wrestler on the Invasion Attack card is found, fetch that wrestler's page (e.g., "Akebono Tarō - Wikipedia") to confirm hometown/city.

Final answer deferred pending retrieval of the targets above.
hop2_docs: Invasion Attack (2013) | Invasion Attack was a professional wrestling pay-per-view (PPV) promoted by New Japan Pro-Wrestling (NJPW).  The event took place on April 7, 2013, in Tokyo at Ryōgoku Kokugikan.  The event featured nine matches, five of which were contested for championships.  As part of the "invasion" theme, the event featured title matches involving championships from Consejo Mundial de Lucha Libre (CMLL) and the National Wrestling Alliance (NWA) as well as participation from outside workers Akebono and Bob Sapp.  It was the first event under the Invasion Attack name.

Invasion Attack 2014 | Invasion Attack 2014 was a professional wrestling pay-per-view (PPV) promoted by New Japan Pro-Wrestling (NJPW).  The event took place on April 6, 2014, in Tokyo at Ryōgoku Kokugikan.  The event featured nine matches, five of which were contested for championships. The tour was hosted by Jersey All Pro Wrestling (JAPW) and featured all of NJPW's top wrestlers, matches for three of the promotion's four championships and the introduction of a new fifth title.

Invasion Attack 2015 | Invasion Attack 2015 was a professional wrestling pay-per-view (PPV) promoted by New Japan Pro-Wrestling (NJPW).  The event took place on April 5, 2015, in Tokyo at Ryōgoku Kokugikan and featured nine matches, four of which were contested for championships.  In addition to airing worldwide through NJPW's internet streaming site, NJPW World, the event also aired in Japan as a regular PPV through SKY PerfecTV! 's Sukachan service.  It was the third event under the Invasion Attack name.

Invasion Attack 2016 | Invasion Attack 2016 was a professional wrestling pay-per-view (PPV) promoted by New Japan Pro-Wrestling (NJPW).  The event took place on April 10, 2016, in Tokyo at Ryōgoku Kokugikan, and featured nine matches, six of which were contested for championships.  The main event saw the winner of the 2016 New Japan Cup, Tetsuya Naito defeat Kazuchika Okada for the IWGP Heavyweight Championship.  The event aired worldwide on NJPW World and featured English commentary, provided by Kevin Kelly and Steve Corino.  In addition, the event also aired in Japan as a PPV through SKY PerfecTV! .  Invasion Attack 2016 was the fourth and last event under the Invasion Attack name, before the event was renamed Sakura Genesis in 2017.

Akebono scale | The Akebono scale is a musical scale commonly used in traditional Japanese music.  Akebono and the Diatonic scale use the same intervals, but Akebono has no fixed tonic; as such, any Akebono note can be the tonic.

JDS Akebono (DE-201) | The JDS "Akebono" (DE 201) was a destroyer escort (or frigate) of the Japanese Maritime Self-Defense Force.  "Akebono" was one of the first indigenous Japanese warships to be built following World War II.  "Akebono" was laid down in 1954 as a steam turbine powered "B type" ASW escort, the only ship of its class, for comparison with two similar diesel powered ships, the "Ikazuchi"-class destroyer escort s. "Akebono" entered service in 1956 and remained in use until 1976.
response.summary_2: Akebono Tarō is from Waimānalo, Hawaii, and Invasion Attack (2013) featured participation from Akebono.
